coredata.dc:title: Invasive plant-derived carbon dots and carbon black co-deposited basalt fiber fabric as an efficient solar interface evaporator for high salinity water purification

coredata.dc:description: As global freshwater resources become increasingly scarce, developing novel, highly efficient, and robust photothermal materials for solar interfacial evaporation is vital for alleviating the freshwater crisis. This study proposes an innovative utilization of the leaves from an invasive plant species, Rhus typhina L., as a carbon source to prepare carbon dots (CDs) through hydrothermal synthesis and freeze-drying process. The CDs were then combined with carbon black (CB) and deposited onto basalt fabric (BF) via an immersion coating method to obtain a novel two-dimensional CDs/CB@BF photothermal fabric. The surface morphology, elemental composition, hydrophilicity, and light absorption performance of the prepared fabric were investigated using various advanced characterization techniques. The results showed that the developed material possesses an excellent light absorption property, low evaporation enthalpy (1681 J·g−1), and superior capability for water transportation. More importantly, the CDs/CB@BF evaporator exhibited a remarkable evaporation rate of 2.05 kg·m−2·h−1 in pure water and a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 95.72 % under 1 sun irradiation. Apart from that, the evaporator demonstrated outstanding stability in multiple cyclic tests, and the major ion concentrations after evaporation met the standard for drinking water. The present study provides a new strategy for resourceful utilization and controlling invasive plant species. It also offers an efficient and sustainable way to prepare photothermal materials for solar-driven desalination technologies with broad application prospects.

head.ce:title: Invasive plant-derived carbon dots and carbon black co-deposited basalt fiber fabric as an efficient solar interface evaporator for high salinity water purification

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As global freshwater resources become increasingly scarce, developing novel, highly efficient, and robust photothermal materials for solar interfacial evaporation is vital for alleviating the freshwater crisis. This study proposes an innovative utilization of the leaves from an invasive plant species, Rhus typhina L., as a carbon source to prepare carbon dots (CDs) through hydrothermal synthesis and freeze-drying process. The CDs were then combined with carbon black (CB) and deposited onto basalt fabric (BF) via an immersion coating method to obtain a novel two-dimensional CDs/CB@BF photothermal fabric. The surface morphology, elemental composition, hydrophilicity, and light absorption performance of the prepared fabric were investigated using various advanced characterization techniques. The results showed that the developed material possesses an excellent light absorption property, low evaporation enthalpy (1681 J·g−1), and superior capability for water transportation. More importantly, the CDs/CB@BF evaporator exhibited a remarkable evaporation rate of 2.05 kg·m−2·h−1 in pure water and a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 95.72 % under 1 sun irradiation. Apart from that, the evaporator demonstrated outstanding stability in multiple cyclic tests, and the major ion concentrations after evaporation met the standard for drinking water. The present study provides a new strategy for resourceful utilization and controlling invasive plant species. It also offers an efficient and sustainable way to prepare photothermal materials for solar-driven desalination technologies with broad application prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is an essential resource for human survival and development. However, the quantity of fresh water distributed on the Earth's surface is limited and incapable of fulfilling the increasing demand [1]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop innovative methods to tackle issues related to the freshwater supply [2]. Seawater constitutes 96.5 % of the Earth's water resources, and converting seawater into freshwater through desalination technology provides a potential solution to address the global water shortage issues [3]. Traditional desalination technologies, such as membrane distillation and mechanical vapor compression, typically require complicated equipment, substantial investment costs, and significant energy consumption, which limit their broad applications [4]. Pursuing green and economic desalination technology has become a focal point of current research to address these challenges. Solar energy, as an easy and renewable clean resource, can reduce energy consumption and operational costs in the desalination process and promote the sustainable development of water and energy resources [5]. To minimize the heat loss during the solar evaporation of seawater and enhance the efficiency of solar energy utilization, Ghasemi et al. [6] proposed solar interfacial evaporation (SIE) technology by concentrating heat at the liquid–gas interface to obtain freshwater. The whole process was featured by the high photothermal conversion efficiency, low cost, and non-pollution [7].

In SIE technology, photothermal materials are pivotal in determining system efficiency [8]. Over recent years, researchers have developed various photothermal materials, including carbon-based materials [9–11], semiconductors [12], plasmonic metals [13], and polymers [14], to enhance solar spectrum absorption and thereby achieve efficient steam generation at the liquid–gas interface. However, when applied to high-salinity wastewater treatment, salt accumulation degrades the performance of photothermal materials and clogs water transport channels, significantly reducing evaporation efficiency [15]. To enhance the salt tolerance of evaporators, researchers have primarily focused on optimizing structural design and improving material hydrophilicity. For instance, Xiao et al. [16] combined Janus and superhydrophilic cellulose powder, which not only has excellent light absorption and thermal management but also excellent water transport, enabling stable desalination in high-salinity water. Similarly, Ren et al. [17] designed an asymmetric Janus structure evaporator and utilized convection-induced steam diffusion to achieve long-term stability in high-salinity water. Ge et al. [18] proposed a self-regulating strategy for spherical photothermal evaporators, where the rotation of floating spheres timely relocates salt crystals and cleans the photothermal surface, ensuring long-term stable operation even in saturated saline environments. Additionally, Ma et al. [19] introduced hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) groups into evaporators, enhancing water molecule adsorption and forming a continuous hydrogen-bond network. This not only reduces the enthalpy of evaporation but also effectively inhibits salt crystallization at the interface.

It is worth noting that carbon dots (CDs), as a class of carbon-based materials with abundant hydrophilic groups on the surface, have excellent photothermal conversion and thermal insulation properties [15]. In practical applications, CDs can effectively enhance the performance of SIE systems. This combination leverages CDs' light absorption and heat conversion capabilities and their advantages in the adsorption of water molecules and thermal management [20]. For instance, Singh et al. [21] synthesized CDs from phenylenediamine and integrated them with chitosan and cellulose to form a hydrogel SIE system, achieving an evaporation rate of 1.4 kg·m−2·h−1, which demonstrated the practical application potential of CDs in SIE systems. Although these CDs exhibit excellent performance in SIE systems, their preparation costs are typically high, limiting their large-scale applications. To reduce the fabrication costs of CDs, synthesizing CDs from widely available and low-cost biomass as a carbon source is a practical approach. For example, Liu et al. [22] synthesized CDs in situ on pomelo peel through high-temperature carbonization, achieving an evaporation rate of 2.43 kg·m−2·h−1. Moreover, Ji et al. [23] used lignin from light wood as a carbon source to synthesize CDs in situ from wood, achieving an evaporation rate of 2.86 kg·m−2·h−1. While using biomass as a carbon source can reduce the preparation costs of CDs, excessive utilization may disrupt ecosystems. Therefore, it is necessary to find an environmentally friendly method to obtain CDs to achieve sustainable development of SIE technology.

Invasive plant species, including Rhus typhina L. (R. typhina), have been reported to have encroached upon approximately 13 million hectares of global agricultural land [24]. These plants possess competitive advantages in acquiring light, water, and nutrients, and certain species can even secrete chemical substances that inhibit the growth of surrounding vegetation, thereby expanding their populations. Such behaviors erode the diversity and stability of ecosystems and inflict economic damage on local communities [25]. Currently, control measures for invasive plants primarily rely on physical and chemical methods. However, physical methods are often costly and may not achieve comprehensive management, while chemical methods risk causing secondary environmental pollution [26–28]. Therefore, it is essential to develop economically viable approaches for the control of invasive plants, and the valorization of invasive plant biomass represents an effective strategy that can effectively curb their spread while offering economic benefits [29]. For instance, Tuerhong et al. [30] utilized the leaves from R. typhina and Amorpha fruticosa as a carbon source to prepare an activated carbon with an excellent adsorption property for chromium in water, which is capable of converting the harmful hexavalent chromium to the less toxic trivalent form during the adsorption process. Similarly, Zhang et al. [31] extracted cellulose from Solidago canadensis L., cross-linked it with polyethylene glycol to synthesize a cellulose aerogel, and subsequently polymerized dopamine in situ on the surface of aerogel. This resulted in an evaporation rate of 2.05 kg·m−2·h−1 and a photothermal conversion efficiency of 95.5 % in the SIE system. Thus, the resourceful utilization of invasive plants not only effectively control their proliferation and protect ecosystems, but also could generate economic value.

In SIE systems, the water transport layer is a crucial component, which must have excellent water transport and corrosion resistance properties. Basalt fiber (BF) fabric is is made by melting basalt rocks at 1450–1500 °C and then drawing it into fibers. Recently, it has emerged as a promising material in SIE systems due to its exceptional mechanical strength, chemical stability, durability, and water transportation capability [32]. For instance, Wan et al. [33] fabricated a photothermal material by weaving BF into fabrics and subjecting it to high-temperature carbonization, achieving a freshwater evaporation rate of 1.50 kg·m−2·h−1. However, the high-temperature carbonization process may compromise the mechanical integrity of BF. To address the issue of BF's degraded mechanical properties at high temperatures, Zuo et al. [34] used BF as a substrate and in situ grew a nano carbon coating from polyvinyl alcohol on BF, achieving an evaporation rate of 1.79 kg·m−2·h−1 in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Nevertheless, using this method, the preparation of photothermal materials is energy-intensive and not conducive to large-scale fabrication. Song et al. [35] employed an immersion coating method to grow carbon nanotubes on the surface of BF, with the objective of reducing energy consumption and facilitating low-cost production of photothermal fabrics. However, the resulting evaporation rate in pure water was only 1.53 kg·m−2·h−1, which represents a significant limitation in terms of its potential for large-scale freshwater production. Therefore, there is an urgent need for further research based on BF to explore simpler fabrication processes for photothermal fabrics and the development of more efficient photothermal materials.

Considering the issues mentioned above, in the present work, we have successfully developed a novel photothermal material composed of CDs/CB deposited on BF (CDs/CB@BF), aiming at resourceful utilization of the biomass of R. typhina and addressing the global freshwater scarcity. At first, CDs were extracted from the leaves of R. typhina through a series of processes, including hydrothermal synthesis, purification, and lyophilization processes. They were then combined with CB under high-temperature and high-pressure conditions to form a CDs/CB composite material, which was subsequently loaded onto the surface of BF via an immersion coating technique. The prepared material, CDs/CB@BF, exhibited excellent light absorption, superior water transportation capability, and a reduced evaporation enthalpy. An evaporation rate of 2.05 kg·m-2·h-1 and a photothermal conversion efficiency of 95.72 % were obtained under 1 sun irradiation. Furthermore, the material exhibited good physicochemical stability in durability tests and across a range of pH levels, with a stable evaporation rate and no notable decline observed. The material demonstrated exceptional salt tolerance, with an evaporation rate of 1.81 kg·m−2·h−1 in the simulated seawater with a salinity of 3.5 wt%. The treated water exhibited significantly reduced ion concentrations, meeting the drinking water standards set by the World Health Organization (WHO). Additionally, the material effectively purified various wastewaters, including organic dye solutions and oil-in-water emulsions, showing a broad potential for practical applications. An economic analysis indicated that CDs/CB@BF has an effective coefficient of 87.23 g·h−1·$-1, suggesting that investing in this photothermal fabric could yield significant economic returns. This study provides a new strategy for using invasive plant biomass and offers an effective solution to the freshwater scarcity problem, with substantial promise for practical applications.

The invasive R. typhina leaves were collected in Urumqi City, Xinjiang, China. Dialysis bags with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da were procured from RuiTaibio Technology Co., Ltd. (RuiTaibio, Beijing, China). BF with a diameter of 13 μm and a 300 m2·g-1 mass was obtained from Guizhou Shixin Basalt Technology Co., Ltd. (Liupanshui City, Guizhou, China). CB was purchased from Jiangsu Xianfeng Nano Material Technology Co., Ltd. (XFNANO, Jiangsu, China). Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) were provided by Arkema Co., Ltd. (Paris, France). Graphene (GR) was purchased from Asbury Carbons, Inc. (Asbury, USA). Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and sodium alginate (SA) were acquired from the Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was supplied by BASF Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Anhydrous calcium chloride (CaCl2) was obtained from Xinbote Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Polyethylene foam was sourced from a local market in Urumqi city, Xinjiang, China. All reagents used were of analytical grade and did not require further purification. Deionized water with a resistivity higher than 18.2 MΩ·cm was used in the experiment and obtained through a laboratory water purification system (Eco-S15, Hitech, China).

The extraction of CDs consisted of the following steps (Fig. 1 A). First, the leaves of R. typhina were thoroughly cleaned with ultrapure water and allowed to air dry naturally. Subsequently, the dried leaves were pulverized into a fine powder using a grinder and sifted through a 100-mesh screen for later use. A quantity of 2 g of the powdered leaf was measured and poured into a 100 mL beaker, and 60 mL of water was added. The resulting suspension was then subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min in a cleaning instrument to ensure good dispersion. The mixture was then transferred to a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed, and heated in an electric thermal constant temperature convection oven at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the autoclave would cool naturally to room temperature, yielding a brown liquid. This brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to separate impurities and large particles. After that, the supernatant containing the CDs was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 72 h using a 500 Da dialysis bag. A 0.2 μm water filter was employed to filter the dialyzed solution. The purified CDs solution was then freeze-dried at −40 °C. Finally, the prepared CDs were collected for further utilization.

The preparation process of CDs/CB dispersions includes the following steps (Fig. 1B). First, 0.2 g of CDs powder was added into a 200 mL beaker, followed by 100 mL of ultrapure. The mixture was then dispersed using ultrasonication for 5 min. Subsequently, 3 g of CB was introduced, and then the mixture was further dispersed using ultrasonication for an additional 5 min while continuously stirring with a glass rod. The resulting suspension was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, sealed, and placed into an electric thermal constant temperature convection oven. After that, the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After the reaction, the autoclave would be cooled naturally to room temperature. Then, the content was filtered through vacuum filtration to separate the liquid from the solid. The black powder collected on the filter paper was transferred into an electric thermal constant temperature convection oven and dried at 60 °C for 6 h. The obtained product was kept for subsequent use. Next, 0.1 g of CTAB would be dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water and placed into a beaker in an ultrasonic cleaner until the CTAB was completely dissolved. Then, 0.3 g of SA was slowly added into the beaker containing an aliquot of CTAB solution, ensuring uniform mixing of CTAB and SA. Subsequently, under ultrasonication, 0.3 g of CDs/CB was slowly added into the CTAB-SA mixture, stirred with a glass rod for 1 min, and then ultrasonicated for 15 min to obtain a preliminary mixture. To achieve a stable dispersion, the CDs/CB mixture was ultrasonicated in an ultrasonic cell disruptor for 1 h, resulting in the final CDs/CB dispersion.

The fabrication procedures of CDs/CB@BF are illustrated in Fig. 1C. Initially, the BF was cleaned with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol, followed by natural air drying. Subsequently, the central portion of the BF (20 cm in length and 6 cm in width) was immersed into a container, which contained a NaOH solution with a concentration of 2 mol·L-1. The container was sealed and subjected to thermal treatment at 90°C for 3 h. Afterward, the BF was retrieved, the surface of which would be continuously rinsed with ultrapure water until the rinse liquid was neutral. After that, the cleaned BF was dried in a thermal convection oven at 60°C for 1 h. The prepared CDs/CB dispersion was poured into a square petri dish, and the central part of the BF was immersed into the dispersion for 2 min on each side, followed by drying in a convection oven at 85°C. This whole process was repeated 5 times to ensure uniform coating of CDs/CB onto the BF. Subsequently, the BF loaded with CDs/CB was immersed into a CaCl2 solution with a concentration of 0.1 mol·L-1 for cross-linking for 10 h. Upon completion, the surface of the BF was rinsed with ultrapure water to remove excess CaCl2 and then dried in an oven at 60°C for 1 h. Finally, the BF-based photothermal fabric CDs/CB@BF was obtained. As a control experiment, CDs@BF and CB@BF were also prepared, following the same procedure, except that CDs/CB were replaced by CDs or CB alone.

In the present study, the fluorescent spectrum of CDs was characterized using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (SR-3031-A, ANDOR, UK) under 365 nm excitation. The morphologies of the CDs, CB, and CDs/CB were observed under transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Specifically, these CD powders were dispersed into ultrapure water to prepare a solution with a 0.1 mg·mL-1 concentration. Then, 10 μL of the CDs solution was dropped onto an ultrathin carbon-coated copper grid and allowed to dry completely for TEM observation. The surface morphology of raw BF, CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF was investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7610F Plus, JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 5.0 kV. Samples were mounted on an aluminum specimen stage and coated with a platinum layer using an ion sputter coater (MC1000, Hitachi, Japan) to enhance the conductivity. The crystal structures of CDs, CB, and CDs/CB were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, D8 Advance, Germany). Additionally, the elemental compositions and the surface structures of CDs, CB, and CDs/CB were characterized in detail using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, X-Flash SDD 5010, Bruker, USA). Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Bruker, VERTEX 70, Germany) confirmed the successful loading of CDs, CB, and CDs/CB onto BF. Furthermore, a contact angle goniometer (SL200KS, Kino, US) was used to measure the water contact angles of raw BF, CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF to assess their hydrophilicity. A UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (UH5700, Hitachi, Japan) was employed to test the absorption spectra of the samples as mentioned above in the wavelength range of 200–2500 nm. The changes in the surface temperature of the photothermal fabric under illumination were monitored using an infrared thermal imager (UTI320E, UNI-T, China). The primary ion concentrations (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+) in the simulated seawater before and after evaporation were determined using an inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP, Agilent Technologies 5900, ICP-OES, USA). Finally, the purity of the water was assessed using a multimeter with a fixed electrode distance (UT39C, UNI-T, China).

During indoor tests, a high-salinity seawater sample was prepared in accordance with the ASTM D1141-2021 standard, with a pH of 8.61, a color degree of 19.8C, and a temperature of 22.9 °C. Excluding heavy metals, the salinity was approximately 35,000 mg·L-1. The detailed composition of the simulated seawater used in this work is presented in Table S1.

The evaporation performance of the CDs/CB@BF fabric was investigated using a laboratory-fabricated evaporator, as depicted in Fig. S1. The SIE unit comprises a solar simulator, an electronic balance, two identically sized water storage tanks, PS foam, and BF photothermal material. Both ends of the photothermal fabric with effective area of 6.0 cm × 3.5 cm dimensions were immersed into the water storage tanks (10.0 cm × 3.0 cm × 7.0 cm) positioned at the center of the electronic balance. Water was transported to the photothermal fabric interface via capillary action, and the electronic balance (JDJS-A7 + Dmin, J-SKY, China) was used to record the mass changes of water during the evaporation tests. The water storage tanks were entirely covered with PS foam to prevent significant heat loss, while the CDs/CB@BF fabric was fully exposed to the vertical light path of the solar simulator. A solar simulator (500Solar-500, NBeT, China) was utilized to simulate sunlight exposure, with an output solar flux set at 1 kW·m−2 (equivalent to 1 sun irradiation). Before each test, the light intensity was calibrated using a handheld digital power meter (TES-132, TES, China). The evaporation performance and the photothermal conversion efficiency of the CDs/CB@BF evaporator were studied by adjusting the solar irradiation (0.5, 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0 sun), and its desalination capability was tested through evaporation in high-salinity wastewater. The evaporation rate and the photothermal conversion efficiency were calculated using Equations (1)–(3) [36]:

μ 1
where m is the mass of the evaporated water (kg), S is the effective area of the evaporator (0.0021 m2), t is the time of solar irradiation (h), η is the photothermal conversion efficiency, μ is the rate of water evaporation, ΔHv is the enthalpy of evaporation of water in the sample, q0 is the intensity of a sun's radiation, C0pt is number of suns, ΔH1 is the enthalpy of evaporation of pure water (2440 J·g−1), μ0 is the dark evaporation rate of pure water, μ1 is the dark evaporation rate of the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CDs/CB@BF evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: In order to utilize invasive plants, CDs were extracted from R. typhina and combined with CB and BF to prepare the CDs/CB@BF evaporator (Fig. 2 A). This whole process involves the impregnation and coating method for the deposition of CDs/CB and SA onto the BF substrate, resulting in a photothermal fabric with excellent water supply capability, superior photothermal conversion efficiency, and reduced evaporation enthalpy. The carboxyl groups of SA can physically cross-link with Ca2+ to form a hydrogel, thereby enhancing the BF's mechanical strength and water transportation performance and preventing the detachment of CDs/CB from the BF surface. The BF substrate serves a dual role in this SIE system: on the one hand, it transports water to the gas–liquid interface through capillary action. On the other hand, the central part of the BF loaded with CDs/CB captures sunlight to heat the water at the gas–liquid interface, facilitating water vapor production. As can be seen from Fig. 2B, the aqueous solution of CDs in the leaves of the torch tree is brownish yellow under natural light, clear and transparent without turbidity, indicating its good water solubility. In the dark environment, excited by 365 nm ultraviolet light, the aqueous solution of CDs in the torch leaves emits blue fluorescence at 465 nm.

The morphology of the CDs was further characterized using TEM. As shown in Fig. 2C, the CDs exhibit a quasi-spherical particle shape with excellent dispersibility. Further analysis with ImageJ software revealed a 3–10 nm particle size distribution for the CDs, with an average diameter of 6.86 nm. In Fig. 2D, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images distinctly display the lattice fringes of CDs. Utilizing Gatan Digital Micrograph software for precise measurement, the lattice spacing of CDs was determined to be 0.22 nm, which is in high agreement with the lattice spacing of the graphite C (100) plane, indicating the presence of graphitic sp2 − bonded carbon clusters in the CDs. This unique lattice structure can efficiently absorb incident light and induce lattice vibrations, thereby achieving good photothermal conversion. When sunlight strikes this lattice structure, it triggers lattice vibrations that generate thermal energy and excites electrons for transition between different energy levels. During this process, energy losses associated with electron transitions are also converted into thermal energy. All the generated thermal energy converges to provide an efficient photothermal driving force for interfacial evaporation, thereby significantly enhancing the efficiency of solar-driven evaporation [37]. Fig. 2E presents the TEM image of CB, revealing particle sizes ranging from 30 to 50 nm. These nanoparticles enhance light absorption through multiple scattering and absorption effects, enabling efficient solar energy conversion into thermal energy [38]. Fig. 2F shows the TEM image of CDs/CB@BF, where it can be seen that CDs are attached to CB, and there are no significant changes in the size and surface morphology of CDs and CB. Subsequently, the surface morphology of the prepared raw BF, CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF photothermal fabric was further examined using SEM. The SEM image of raw BF in Fig. 2G shows a very smooth surface, and the image of CDs@BF in Fig. 2H displays a multitude of tiny pores on the surface, which are conducive to enhancing heat absorption performance. Further observation of Fig. 2I and Fig. 2J revealed that the introduction of CDs not only increases the roughness and porosity of the photothermal fabric surface but also significantly increases the loading of photothermal materials on the CDs/CB@BF surface. This phenomenon may stem from increased functional groups on the CB surface due to the introduction of CDs. These functional groups react with the silicate structure on the surface of BF to form covalent bonds, which enhance the adhesion of CB to the fabric and allow more CB to adhere to the fabric surface [35].

A detailed analysis of the crystal structures of CDs, CB, and the CDs/CB composite material was conducted using XRD analysis, with the resulting diffraction patterns as presented in Fig. 3 A. At a diffraction angle (2θ) of approximately 44°, the CDs exhibit a characteristic peak attributed to the graphite phase C (100) crystal plane, which is consistent with the lattice fringe analysis observed by HRTEM. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of the CDs/CB composite material is similar in morphology to that of the CDs, indicating a significant impact of the CDs on the crystal structure of the CB, which is likely related to the dispersion and interaction of the CDs on the CB surface. FTIR was employed to ascertain further the functional groups on the surfaces of the samples. Fig. 3B displays the ATR-FTIR spectra of raw BF, CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF, aligning with the expected outcomes. The surface of raw BF exhibits distinct peaks due to the silicate skeleton, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O-Si-O at 680 cm−1 and Si-O at 902 cm−1. Although other photothermal fabrics also possess these characteristic peaks, they are notably reduced in intensity, indicating successful coating adherence to the surface of the fabric. In addition to raw BF, CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF all exhibit symmetric stretching vibrations of COO– at 1423 cm−1, representing the existence of SA. Moreover, the characteristic peak of CDs at 1610 cm−1, corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibration of COO–, is attributed to the carboxylic acid group (COOH) unique to CDs. This group is not present on the surface of CB. Hence, CDs@BF and CDs/CB@BF display this peak in their spectra, whereas CB@BF does not, confirming the successful grafting of CDs onto the CDs/CB. Consequently, the CDs/CB@BF exhibits asymmetric stretching vibrations of COO–. CDs@BF and CDs/CB@BF show –OH stretching vibrations in the 3180–3600 cm-1 range, with a notably diminished signal for CDs/CB@BF, while CB@BF lacks this characteristic peak.

XPS was employed to analyze the elemental compositions and the surface structures of CDs, CB, and the CDs/CB composite material. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the CDs comprise 59.89 % C and 40.11 % O elements. Fig. 4B and Fig. 4C illustrate C and O bonding configurations in the CDs, which mainly include C–C/C=C, C-OH, O-C-O/C=O, and O=C-O chemical bonds. Through quantitative analysis of the relative content of C and O in the CDs, CB, and CDs/CB, it was calculated that 83.02 % of the CDs had been successfully grafted onto the CB. Compared to the C 1 s XPS spectrum of CB (Fig. 4E), the C 1 s XPS spectrum of CDs/CB (Fig. 4H) shows a relative decrease in the intensity of the C–C/C=C bond (284.8 eV), an increase in the relative content of the O-C-O/C=O bond (286.7 eV), and the emergence of a new characteristic peak for the O=C-O bond (289.2 eV). Furthermore, compared to the O 1 s XPS spectrum of CB (Fig. 4F), the O 1 s XPS spectrum of CDs/CB (Fig. 4I) exhibits a significant increase in the relative content of the C=O bond (532.1 eV). The characteristic peak of the O=C-O bond (533.2 eV) also appears in the O 1 s XPS spectrum of CDs/CB, which corresponds with the XPS as mentioned above results. These changes indicate that the CDs have been successfully grafted onto the surface of CB. Further analysis confirmed that the introduction of CDs significantly increases the number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the CB surface, which is beneficial for forming covalent bonds with the silicate structure, thereby enhancing the adhesion between CB and BF, allowing for a more significant loading of CDs/CB onto the BF surface. This result is consistent with the SEM observations of a noticeable increase in photothermal material on the CDs/CB@BF surface. Additionally, the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs/CB composite material also contribute to the hydroxylation process and the activation of water molecules, indicating that CDs play a crucial role in regulating the composition of CDs/CB@BF [36].

The water contact angle is crucial for measuring surface wettability/hydrophilicity, influencing water's wetting and spreading behavior on the solar energy absorption layer. A smaller contact angle facilitates the spreading of water molecules at the evaporation interface, thereby increasing the evaporation area and enhancing the photothermal conversion efficiency. Fig. 5 presents the results of the water contact angle tests for raw BF, CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF. From Fig. 5, it can be observed that raw BF exhibits a large initial water contact angle and fails to spread completely within 1.2 s, indicating relatively weak hydrophilicity compared to other surfaces. In contrast, the water contact angles of CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF are significantly reduced at 0 s, attributed to the introduction of the SA-CaCl2 cross-linking system, which markedly enhances the hydrophilic properties of the material. Furthermore, the water contact angle of CDs/CB@BF is smaller than that of CB@BF, likely due to the incorporation of CDs onto the CB surface, which introduces a wealth of -OH and -COOH groups among other oxygen-containing functional groups. These oxygen-containing functional groups form a hydrogen bond network with water molecules, effectively reducing the water contact angle on the surface of CDs/CB@BF, thereby enhancing its hydrophilicity.

Light absorption capacity is an essential factor affecting the evaporation efficiency of SIE materials. The UV–Vis-NIR spectroscopy was utilized to assess the light absorption performance of raw BF, CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF. The results are presented in Fig. 6 A, compared to CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF, the raw BF exhibits weaker sunlight absorption capability, although it still demonstrates specific spectral absorption characteristics. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to the natural light yellowish hue of BF, which confers a certain degree of light absorption. However, its smooth surface is prone to light reflection, detrimental to effective light absorption [35]. The surface of CDs is rich in oxygen-containing functional groups such as -COOH and -OH groups, which can form stronger hydrogen bonds with water molecules compared to carbonyl and epoxy groups. This enhances the hydrophilicity of the material, facilitating the adsorption and transport of water molecules on the material surface, thereby significantly improving the light-to-vapor conversion efficiency [39]. Additionally, these oxygen-containing functional groups introduce discrete energy levels that facilitate the transfer of electrons from the “core” to the “shell,” thereby enhancing the absorption of near-infrared light [40]. Consequently, CDs@BF exhibits superior light-harvesting capability in the near-infrared region compared to pristine BF. The photothermal conversion mechanism of CB is analogous to that of CDs, primarily involving the absorption of incident photons and the conversion of photon energy into electronic excited states. Subsequently, the excited electrons transfer energy to the lattice via non-radiative relaxation processes, leading to localized temperature increases. CB possesses an extremely broad absorption spectrum, enabling efficient absorption of solar light across the entire spectrum from ultraviolet to infrared. This broad-spectrum absorption endows CB@BF with the ability to capture more solar energy during the photothermal conversion process, thereby significantly enhancing photothermal conversion efficiency. In the CDs/CB@BF composite, the incorporation of CDs markedly improves the dispersion of CB. Enhanced dispersion facilitates uniform absorption and transport of photons within the material, reducing photon reflection and scattering losses. Moreover, the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs may introduce additional defect states that serve as trapping centers for photogenerated charge carriers, further enhancing light-harvesting efficiency. As a result, CDs/CB@BF exhibits superior light absorption performance compared to CB@BF alone, demonstrating higher photothermal conversion efficiency and faster light response capabilities.

Fig. 6B illustrates the construction of an SIE setup with photothermal fabrics, where water is stored in acrylic glass tanks and transported to the photothermal interface via capillary action of the BF for desalination purposes, with polystyrene foam above the tanks to prevent heat loss during the experiment. Fig. 6C depicts the temperature changes on CB@BF and CDs/CB@BF surfaces under dry conditions and 1 sun irradiation for 0–600 s. The results show that the maximum surface temperature of CDs/CB@BF could reach 95.1°C, while that of CB@BF only reached 91.6°C, demonstrating a significant advantage of CDs/CB@BF regarding light response and heat conversion rate. Fig. 6D presents the surface temperature changes of CB@BF and CDs/CB@BF during the water evaporation experiments, with surface temperatures rising from 20 °C to 40.1 °C and 43.4 °C, respectively. Although the surface temperatures of the samples in the wet state are lower than in the dry state, the overall surface temperature of CDs/CB@BF remains higher than that of CB@BF, which can be attributed to three potential factors: Firstly, SEM results reveal CDs/CB@BF has a highly rough and porous structure that facilitates multiple internal reflections of light. Secondly, XPS results indicate that the hydroxyl and aldehyde functional groups present in CDs/CB can form covalent bonds with the silicate system in BF, enhancing the adhesion of CDs/CB to BF and promoting a more significant loading of CDs/CB on the BF surface. Thirdly, the hydrogen bond network formed between the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs/CB and water molecules helps to reduce the evaporation enthalpy of water, decreasing the heat required for the phase change of water from liquid to vapor on the material surface [41].

To explore an optimal photothermal material, GR, CNTs, and CB were individually deposited onto BF via an immersion coating method, and three varieties of photothermal materials, GR@BF, CNTs@BF, and CB@BF, were obtained and then subjected to interfacial water evaporation tests. As depicted in Fig. 7 A, GR@BF, CNTs@BF, and CB@BF achieved an evaporation rate of 1.50, 1.52, and 1.62 kg·m−2·h−1, respectively. CB exhibits superior photothermal performance with the highest evaporation rate of 1.62 kg·m−2·h−1. Furthermore, the evaporation rates of BF coated with different concentrations of CB were investigated under 1 sun irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7B, the evaporation rates of BF coated with 0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, and 0.3 wt% CB solutions demonstrate an increasing trend, and the maximum evaporation rate of 1.62 kg·m−2·h−1 was achieved at a CB concentration of 0.3 wt%. Beyond this concentration, the evaporation rate declines, maybe due to the formation of a shell layer on the BF surface at excessively high CB concentrations, leading to increased light reflection and reduced water supply rate from BF, thereby decreasing the evaporation rate of CB@BF (Fig. 7D). Additionally, the optimal dosage of CDs was investigated by preparing 6 samples with different mass ratios of CDs to CB of 0, 1:30, 1:15, 1:10, 1:7.5, and 1:5. As illustrated in Fig. 7C, the samples with a mass ratio of 1:15 exhibited the highest evaporation rate of 2.05 kg·m−2·h−1. This finding suggests that when the CDs content is low, insufficient adsorption and transport of water molecules limit the water supply rate. Additionally, the lack of functional groups to form hydrogen-bond networks with water molecules results in an inconspicuous reduction in the enthalpy of vaporization, leading to increased heat loss and reduced evaporation efficiency. Conversely, when the CDs content is excessive, although the abundant functional groups enhance water molecule adsorption, the overabundance of CDs can cover the CB surface, obstructing light absorption and transfer, thereby reducing photothermal conversion efficiency and ultimately decreasing the evaporation rate.

In this study, the evaporation performance of various samples under 1 sun irradiation was assessed using the SIE device constructed as depicted in Fig. 6B. The results, as illustrated in Fig. 8 A, demonstrate that the evaporation rates of water, raw BF, CDs@BF, CB@BF, and CDs/CB@BF were 0.45, 1.24, 1.38, 1.57, and 2.05 kg·m−2·h−1, respectively. The CDs/CB@BF exhibits superior evaporation performance among all tested samples, attributed to its rapid water transportation capability, exceptional light absorption characteristic, and reduced evaporation enthalpy. Furthermore, the equivalent evaporation enthalpy of each sample was calculated using Equation (3), with detailed data presented in Table S2. The CB@BF exhibited an equivalent evaporation enthalpy of 1991 J·g−1, while the CDs/CB@BF showed a decreased value of 1681 J·g−1. This reduction is attributed to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs/CB@BF, which interact with water molecules to form hydrogen bonds, thereby promoting the formation of weak hydrogen-bonded intermediate water and effectively reducing the energy requirement for water evaporation [41]. Additionally, the photothermal conversion efficiency of the samples was determined using Equation (2), with the results summarized in Fig. 8A and Table S2. A comparative analysis of the evaporation performance and the photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs/CB@BF with those reported in the existing literature is presented in Fig. 8B and Table S3. The CDs/CB@BF developed in this study outperforms most of the previously reported samples in terms of evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency, thereby validating its potential and advantages in solar-driven water evaporation applications [31,33–35,37,42–45].

The evaporation performance of CB@BF and CDs/CB@BF was investigated under different solar irradiance intensities of 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 suns. Fig. 9 A illustrates the evaporation rates and the photothermal efficiencies of CDs/CB@BF under different sunlight radiation intensities. It can be observed that the evaporation rates of CDs/CB@BF at 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 suns irradiation were 1.18 kg·m−2·h−1, 2.05 kg·m−2·h−1, 2.32 kg·m−2·h−1, and 3.18 kg·m−2·h−1, with corresponding photothermal efficiencies of 110.20 %, 95.72 %, 72.22 %, and 74.24 %, respectively. Fig. 9B presents CB@BF's evaporation rates and the photothermal conversion efficiencies of CB@BF under different solar light intensities. Although the evaporation rate of CB@BF increases with increasing light intensity, its overall evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency are lower than that of CDs/CB@BF, attributed to the weaker light absorption capacity of CB@BF. As the light intensity increases, a significant portion of the light is reflected, resulting in energy loss. In addition, the evaporator exhibits a photothermal conversion efficiency of more than 100 % under low solar radiation. Thermal energy from the environment can compensate for the insufficiency of solar energy, thereby increasing the overall energy input of the system. This additional thermal energy input can promote the rise of temperature in the photothermal material, thereby enhancing evaporation efficiency.

For SIE technology, photothermal materials not only possess excellent capabilities of water transportation, high evaporation rates, and superior photothermal conversion efficiencies but also should have high stabilities, which is an important criterion. As depicted in Fig. 10 A, the 12-cycle stability tests conducted with CDs/CB@BF under 1 sun irradiation indicate that the CDs/CB@BF evaporation rate does not significantly decrease after consecutive 12 cycles, confirming its excellent stability. Furthermore, to assess the stability of CDs/CB@BF under various pH conditions, this study employed a tTris-HCl buffer system, adjusting the pH to 3, 6, 9, and 12 by adding NaOH or HCl solutions. As shown in Fig. 10B, the CDs/CB@BF maintained a stable evaporation rate across the pH range of 3 to 12, comparable to its performance with pure water, demonstrating that its evaporation properties are unaffected by acidity or alkalinity. These findings sufficiently prove the remarkable chemical stability of CDs/CB@BF.

In studying SIE technology for seawater desalination, maintaining a high evaporation rate in high-salinity seawater necessitates materials with exceptional salt tolerance. In this research, the salt tolerance performance of the CDs/CB@BF evaporator was evaluated under 1 sun irradiation using high-salinity simulated seawater (the specific composition is shown in Table S1). The experimental results indicated that during the continuous 8-hour desalination process, the evaporation rate of the evaporator remained stable above 1.70 kg·m−2·h−1 without any significant decline (Fig. 11 A). Notably, from the 4th hour onwards, significant salt crystallization occurred on the evaporator surface, and the number of salt crystals increased over time. However, as depicted in Fig. 11A, the evaporation rate of the evaporator was hardly affected. After a natural self-cleaning process overnight, the salt on the evaporator surface completely dissolved (Fig. 11B). Furthermore, to test the self-cleaning ability of the evaporator, 0.5 g of NaCl was placed on the surface of the CDs/CB@BF evaporator to allow the salt to redissolve into the simulated seawater. As shown in Fig. 11C, after 170 min, the salt on the evaporator surface wholly dissolved into the water. To assess the stability of the evaporator, a continuous 5-day evaporation performance test was conducted using this evaporator, with 8 h of seawater desalination each day. The results showed that the evaporation rate remained stable throughout the test period without any significant changes (Fig. 11D). The evaporator's outstanding salt tolerance and stability are primarily attributed to two factors. First, BF has inherent hydrophilicity and excellent resistance to seawater corrosion, and the cross-linked structure formed by SA and CaCl2 on its surface further enhances the hydrophilicity of the evaporator, thereby improving water transport efficiency. Second, the introduction of CDs provides a wealth of oxygen-containing functional groups, which can cross-link with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in SA, thereby enhancing the stability and hydrophilicity of the material, accelerating water transport, and causing the salt deposited on the evaporator surface to redissolve into the water. Subsequently, we used a multimeter with a fixed electrode distance to compare the purity of four different water samples (seawater, tap water, desalinate water, and ultrapure water). The higher the ohm value, the lower the ion content in the water samples, as illustrated in Fig. 11E, where the ion content in the desalinated seawater is close to that in the ultrapure water. After that, ICP tests were conducted to measure the changes in ion concentrations (Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+) in the simulated seawater before and after the evaporation process, and the results are shown in Fig. 11F. Compared to the untreated water, the ion concentrations in the treated water are significantly reduced by four orders of magnitude, meeting the drinking water standards set by WHO (concentrations of Na+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+ at 200 mg·L-1, 12 mg·L-1, 30 mg·L-1, 30 mg·L-1, respectively), which indicates that the solar evaporator device with the CDs/CB@BF fabric has practical significance for application in large-scale seawater desalination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fouling resistance test of CDs/CB@BF evaporator
ce:section.ce:para: In real wastewater purification, the oil-in-water emulsion, dye in organic solution, and aqueous dye solution are three major concerns. To assess the fouling resistances of the samples, an oil-in-water emulsion with an oil concentration of 10,000 ppm and a methyl orange dye solution with a dye concentration of 1,000 ppm were prepared, and the evaporation rates of CDs/CB@BF in these media were evaluated as depicted in Fig. 12 A and Fig. 12B. The evaporation rates of CDs/CB@BF in the oil-in-water emulsion and the methyl orange dye solution were measured to be 1.88 kg·m−2·h−1 and 2.12 kg·m−2·h−1, respectively, which were not significantly different from its rate in pure water. The insets display the color changes of the water samples before and after purification, with the color of the purified water disappearing after the condensation of different solution vapors, in contrast to the original simulated wastewater. To further test the fouling resistance of the evaporator, we conducted a continuous 8-hour wastewater purification experiment under 1 sun irradiation, as depicted in Fig. 12C and Fig. 12D. The evaporator maintained a consistent evaporation rate of 1.70 kg·m−2·h−1 throughout the experiment. These findings demonstrate that the SIE system integrated with CDs/CB@BF exhibits superior fouling resistance and is also effective for purifying various types of wastewater.

The experimental results demonstrated that the CDs/CB@BF evaporator outperforms CB@BF in terms of hydrophilicity, evaporation rate, and photothermal conversion efficiency (Fig. 13 ), primarily attributed to the following three main reasons. Firstly, in terms of water transportation, CDs possess hydrophilic functional groups such as -OH and -COOH groups that interact with water molecules through hydrogen bonds, enhancing the affinity of BF for water, and the incorporation of CDs also increases the porosity of CDs/CB@BF, thereby improving the rate of water transportation [46]. Secondly, regarding light absorption, the covalent bonding between the CDs/CB composite and the silicate framework of BF (Fig. 4H–I) ensures robust adhesion and a higher loading density of photothermal materials. This structural integration minimizes interfacial thermal resistance and enhances energy transfer efficiency. The introduction of CDs significantly increases surface roughness and porosity, as evidenced by SEM images (Fig. 2G–J). This hierarchical architecture promotes multiple internal reflections and diffraction of incident light across the solar spectrum (Fig. 6A), thereby maximizing broadband light absorption and minimizing reflective losses. In contrast, CB@BF lacks the synergistic porosity and oxygen-rich functional groups provided by CDs. As a result, a substantial portion of incident light is reflected, leading to inefficient photothermal conversion [36]. Thirdly, concerning evaporation enthalpy, the oxygen-containing groups (–OH, –COOH) on the CDs/CB surface synergize with the -OH groups of the SA matrix to form an extensive hydrogen bond network with water molecules. This network disrupts the intermolecular hydrogen bonds in water, reducing the energy required for water molecules to break free from the liquid phase and enter the vapor phase. In CB@BF, only the –OH groups of SA contribute to water activation, resulting in limited hydrogen bond disruption. Consequently, the reduction in evaporation enthalpy is less pronounced [41].

The evaporation rate and the operating cost are critical parameters in the practical application of the SIE process. Table 1 lists the costs required for a 1 m2 BF-based solar interfacial evaporation material and conducts a cost-benefit analysis of the evaporator based on the theoretical evaporation rate. The synergy factor (ε) can be calculated using the Equation (4) [35]:
(4)
ε = μ / C
where μ is the evaporation rate of the evaporator (kg·m−2·h−1), and C is the cost of photothermal material ($·m−2).

The evaporation rate of the CDs/CB@BF fabric in the simulated seawater can reach 1.81 kg·m−2·h−1 under the condition of 1 sun irradiation, and the price of the primary material is 20.75 $·m−2. Therefore, the effective coefficient of photothermal materials is 87.23 g·h−1·$−1. This vaporizer is moderately priced compared to other reported evaporator costs (Table S4). Moreover, the extraction of CDs from the R. typhina not only provides a sustainable and cost-effective source of carbon dots but also offers significant environmental benefits. Torch tree is known for its invasive nature, which can cause ecological imbalances and economic losses. By utilizing its biomass for the synthesis of CDs, we can effectively control its spread while generating value-added products. This approach aligns with the principles of circular economy and sustainable development, making the CDs/CB@BF evaporator economically viable and environmentally friendly. Overall, the combination of high evaporation efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and environmental benefits makes this evaporator a promising candidate for large-scale applications in solar-driven desalination technologies.

In summary, to achieve the resourceful utilization of invasive plant species that pose a threat to the environment and ecological system, this work focused on extracting the CDs from the leaves of the invasive plant species R. typhina. These CDs were combined with CB as photothermal materials for the SIE process. Using BF as the substrate and water supply layer, an efficient and environmentally friendly CDs/CB@BF solar evaporator was fabricated through an immersion coating method. Physicochemical characterization of CDs, CB, CDs/CB, and the CDs/CB@BF evaporator was conducted using various techniques, confirming the successful preparation of CDs and deposition of functional materials onto BF. The CDs/CB@BF demonstrated an excellent light absorption property, a superior capability of water transportation, and a lower evaporation enthalpy, obtaining high evaporation rates of 2.05 kg·m−2·h−1 in pure water and 1.81 kg·m−2·h−1 in the simulated seawater. Furthermore, the CDs/CB@BF evaporator showed good stability in multiple cyclic tests, and the quality of the water obtained after the interfacial evaporation test in high-salinity simulated seawater met the drinking water standards set by WHO. Economic analysis indicates that the CDs/CB@BF has significant economic feasibility, with an effective coefficient of 87.23 g·h−1·$-1. Overall, this study not only provides new ideas for the resourceful utilization of invasive plant species but also offers an efficient, economical, and environmentally friendly approach for solar-driven desalination technology, holding significant prospects for practical applications in the future.
